coredata.dc:title: Light stimulated room-temperature H2S gas sensing ability of Cl-doped carbon quantum dots supported Ag nanoparticles

coredata.dc:description: Light-activation of gas sensors is recognized as a potential alternative to the traditional thermal-activation that has several drawbacks such as high power consumption. However, strategies to exploit outstanding light-response characteristics of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for light-activated gas sensing are not established effectively. In the present study, Ag nanoparticles, which have good affinity to hydrogen sulfide (H2S) gas molecules, visible-light absorption, and excellent oxygen dissociation ability, are in-situ grown on Cl-doped CQDs (Cl-CQDs) that exhibit strong UV-light absorption and prolonged charge separation. The developed hybrids with strong interfacial connection between Ag and Cl-CQDs (Ag@Cl-CQDs) exhibited exceptional selectivity and sensitivity towards the H2S gas under light irradiation. The sensitivity depends on the photogenerated electrons flow direction at the Ag–Cl-CQDs heterojunction; UV-light (254 nm, 12.7 mW/cm2) facilitated the migration of electrons from Cl-CQDs to Ag, while the visible-light (457 nm, 152 mW/cm2) favored the electrons flow in the opposite direction. The excellent oxygen dissociation ability of Ag nanoparticles is further fueled by the photogenerated electrons migrated from Cl-CQDs and, as a result, UV-light-activated sensor exhibited higher H2S gas response than the sensor under visible-light irradiation or dark condition. The remarkable sensing performance of UV-light-activated Ag@Cl-CQDs is exploited to detect the H2S gas at lower concentrations (2 ppm–200 ppb). The effect of humidity (20–60%) on the device performance is also investigated. Further, the mechanism behind the Ag@Cl-CQDs sensor's superior performance under UV-light irradiation compared to visible-light irradiation is discussed in detail.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Light-activation of gas sensors is recognized as a potential alternative to the traditional thermal-activation that has several drawbacks such as high power consumption. However, strategies to exploit outstanding light-response characteristics of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for light-activated gas sensing are not established effectively. In the present study, Ag nanoparticles, which have good affinity to hydrogen sulfide (H2S) gas molecules, visible-light absorption, and excellent oxygen dissociation ability, are in-situ grown on Cl-doped CQDs (Cl-CQDs) that exhibit strong UV-light absorption and prolonged charge separation. The developed hybrids with strong interfacial connection between Ag and Cl-CQDs (Ag@Cl-CQDs) exhibited exceptional selectivity and sensitivity towards the H2S gas under light irradiation. The sensitivity depends on the photogenerated electrons flow direction at the Ag–Cl-CQDs heterojunction; UV-light (254 nm, 12.7 mW/cm2) facilitated the migration of electrons from Cl-CQDs to Ag, while the visible-light (457 nm, 152 mW/cm2) favored the electrons flow in the opposite direction. The excellent oxygen dissociation ability of Ag nanoparticles is further fueled by the photogenerated electrons migrated from Cl-CQDs and, as a result, UV-light-activated sensor exhibited higher H2S gas response than the sensor under visible-light irradiation or dark condition. The remarkable sensing performance of UV-light-activated Ag@Cl-CQDs is exploited to detect the H2S gas at lower concentrations (2 ppm–200 ppb). The effect of humidity (20–60%) on the device performance is also investigated. Further, the mechanism behind the Ag@Cl-CQDs sensor's superior performance under UV-light irradiation compared to visible-light irradiation is discussed in detail.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the precise detection of toxic gases has been perceived as one of the most significant technological advancements for the people's safety, health monitoring, and environmental protection [1–8]. Hybrid nanostructures that composed of different types of nanomaterial components are attracting significant interest for achieving the envisaged gas sensor characteristics because of their abilities to form heterojunction and integrate multifunctional properties [9–11]. The response of a resistive type gas sensor typically depends on the reaction between target gas molecules and the adsorbed reactive oxygen species on the gas sensor material. Hence, noble metal (Au, Ag, Pt, Pd, etc.) nanoparticles, which are having better oxygen dissociation abilities and reduce the activation energy of gas sensing reactions, are used in combination with typical gas sensing materials in order to achieve the enhanced gas sensing performance [12–21]. However, the supply of heat energy to hybrid nanostructures containing metal nanoparticles is required to overcome the slow gas sensing kinetics. Therefore, heating setup, which leads to high power consumption and device cost, has been a mandatory component in gas sensors. But, the operation of heaters during the gas sensing measurement causes thermal degradation of nearby components in the sensor device. Hence, it is of particular interest to develop alternatives to heaters and bring down the working temperature of sensors to room temperature.

Recently, photonic energy utilization to activate the gas sensing materials has aroused a significant interest [22]. The irradiation of light having energy sufficient to excite the semiconductor or plasmonic metal nanoparticles enhances the gas sensing characteristics of sensor by 1) bringing more internal charge carriers to participate in surface chemical reactions with gas molecules and 2) accelerating the adsorption and desorption kinetics of gas molecules on the sensor's surface [23]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) or graphene quantum dots, an ultra-small fragments of carbon with size below 10 nm, have attracted significant research interest owing to their unique chemical, optical, and electrical properties [24–32]. Lately, CQDs have been a focus of interest for gas sensing application [1,33–36]. The strong light absorption and charge transfer characteristics of CQDs enable them to provide additional charge carriers under light irradiation for the formation of high amounts of reactive oxygen species on the surface of active sensing material. Therefore, the CQDs integration permit the utilization of light irradiation as an alternative to the high temperature for enhancing the performance of gas sensors. However, it is a prerequisite to inhibit the quick charge recombination kinetics of CQDs in order to fully exploit their light responsive characteristics in gas sensing application. The doping of elemental Cl into CQDs (Cl-CQDs) has been demonstrated to prolong the photocharge separation and extend the light absorption from UV to NIR region [37,38]. Hence, it is of significant interest to investigate light-activated gas sensing performance of a hybrid material made from Cl-CQDs and metal nanoparticles.

A malodorous and colorless toxic hydrogen sulfide (H2S) gas is an irritant and a chemical asphyxiant gas that shows adverse effects on human health [39]. At high concentration levels, a few breaths of H2S is sufficient to induce unconsciousness, seizures, respiratory paralysis, coma, and even death [39]. Hence, precise detection of H2S gas is extremely important. It should be noted that the high temperature utilization to enhance the detection efficiency of H2S gas sensor give rise to serious safety concerns such as the flammable nature of H2S gas could cause the fire or explosion and the burning of H2S gas in the presence of oxygen result in the formation of another potentially dangerous sulfur dioxide (SO2) gas. Therefore, room temperature detection of H2S gas is extremely important.

The synergetic effect of Cl-CQDs and metal nanoparticles is advantageous to detect the H2S gas at room temperature by using the light activation energy. In particular, choosing Ag as a metal nanoparticle is promising owing to Ag metal's excellent oxygen dissociation ability and good affinity to H2S gas. But, the simple physical mixing of Cl-CQDs and Ag nanoparticles cannot provide the strong interfacial interaction for efficient charge transfer across the junction. On the other hand, surfactants or organic protecting ligands usually employed for the stable and size controlled synthesis of Ag nanoparticles remain on the surface of Ag nanoparticle and obstruct the gas molecules interaction [40]. Often hectic post synthesis treatments are required to remove ligands from the surface of Ag nanoparticles [40,41]. To avoid these drawbacks, the excellent electron donating ability of Cl-CQDs has been exploited in the present study to reduce Ag+ ions into Ag nanoparticles on the surface of Cl-CQDs (Ag@Cl-CQDs), which facilitates the strong interaction between Ag nanoparticle and Cl-CQDs. The resulting Ag@Cl-CQDs hybrids demonstrated highly efficient H2S gas detection under the irradiation of UV and visible light. Further, the Ag@Cl-CQDs hybrids exhibited excellent selectivity towards H2S gas and retained good sensing ability under humid conditions.

Sucralose (>98%), silver nitrate (AgNO3, ≥99%), and 2,4-dinitrotoluene (97%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). All chemicals were used as received without further purification. A microwave oven (700 W) purchased from Daewoo Electronics Corp. (South Korea) was employed to synthesize Cl-CQDs. Dialyses of crude Cl-CQDs solution was carried out using molecular weight cutoff membranes (1000 Da) obtained from Spectrum Laboratories, Inc. (USA).

The Cl-CQDs were synthesized according to our earlier reported microwave synthesis method [37]. Typically, 100 mL Erlenmeyer flask containing 0.3 g of sucralose dissolved into 5 mL of deionized (DI) water was subjected to microwave treatment for 6 min after wrapping its mouth loosely with polyethylene film. Then, the flask was naturally cooled to room temperature and the crude Cl-CQDs solution was obtained by adding DI water into the flask. The large particles in the crude solution were separated by centrifugation (3000 rpm, 30 min) and the remaining Cl-CQD solution was purified by performing dialysis for 2 days in DI water. Finally, the purified Cl-CQDs solution was freeze-dried under vacuum to obtain solids of Cl-CQDs.

The Ag@Cl-CQD hybrids were synthesized according to our earlier reported photochemical reduction method [37]. Briefly, a quartz cell containing the mixture of 3 mL DI water or ethanol, 7.5 mg Cl-CQDs, and 1.5 mg AgNO3 was irradiated with visible light (300 W Xenon lamp with a λ > 400 nm cut-off filter) for 25 min. The color transformation of the solution from initial pale brown color to dark brown color signifies the formation of Ag nanoparticles on the surface of Cl-CQDs. Free Ag nanoparticles or unreacted ions in the solution were removed by subjecting the resultant solution to centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 10 min) and replacing the solvent with a fresh DI water. Finally, the freeze-drying of the solution produces solid Ag@Cl-CQDs hybrids.

The morphology and size of Ag@Cl-CQDs were analyzed using field-emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM, Tecnai G2 F20 TEM, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted with a Scientific Sigma Probe Multi-2000 spectrometer (Thermo VG Scientific Co., USA). Optical absorption spectra were recorded in the wavelength range of 200–1000 nm with a Optizen Alpha UV/Vis Smart Spectrometer (Mecasys, South Korea). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded by using a L550B luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, UK). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out using a Bruker D2 phaser diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.1542 nm). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was taken using a Nicolet iS10 FTIR spectroscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The Raman spectrum under the 633 nm excitation was acquired at room temperature using a RAMANtouch spectrometer. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements were performed with a XE-100 atomic force microscope (PSIA Inc., South Korea).

The Cl-CQDs or Ag@Cl-CQDs thin film sensor was fabricated using the SiO2/Si substrates decorated with 120 nm thick bar-type Pt electrodes, which are patterned using direct current sputtering through a shadow mask. Before depositing Pt electrodes, the SiO2/Si substrates were washed for 15 min with each solvent such as methanol, water, and acetone, and subsequently dried by blowing N2 gas. The Cl-CQDs or Ag@Cl-CQDs solution was drop-casted on patterned Pt electrodes and air dried at room temperature for 1 day.

The gas sensing performance of Cl-CQDs or Ag@Cl-CQDs thin films were analyzed using picoammeter/voltage source (Keithley 6487). The sensor thin film was fixed in the test chamber at a distance of 1 cm from the gas line and the change in resistance of the sensor film was monitored after exposing it to target gas for 5 min with a specified flow-rate at constant pressure and temperature. To measure the sensing performance under UV (254 nm, 12.7 mW/cm2) or visible (457 nm, 152 mW/cm2) light irradiation conditions, corresponding LED light source was mounted above the quartz window fitted at the top of the test chamber to illuminate the surface of the sensor film during the gas sensing measurements. The concentration of the target gas was controlled using the following equation,

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Ag@Cl-CQDs hybrids synthesis and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain Ag@Cl-CQDs hybrids, the as-prepared Cl-CQDs were mixed with aqueous or ethanolic solution of AgNO3 and irradiated with visible light for 25 min. As shown in Fig. 1a, the pale brown color of the AgNO3 and Cl-CQDs mixture solution was turned into dark brown color after visible light irradiation, an indication for the formation of Ag nanoparticles. In contrast, the pristine AgNO3 solution didn't exhibit any color transformation and, as a result, signified the importance of Cl-CQDs in reducing Ag+ ions into Ag nanoparticles. TEM images of Ag@Cl-CQDs indicated that the average size of Ag nanoparticles was 34 ± 5.8 nm (Figs. S1(c and d)). Further, the strong connection between Ag nanoparticle and Cl-CQDs observed in HR-TEM images ascertained the growth of Ag nanoparticles on the surface of Cl-CQDs (Fig. 1b, c, and Fig. S3). The lattice fringes with d spacing values of 0.20 and 0.23, respectively, corresponded to Ag (200) and Cl-CQDs (100) were also identified in the HR-TEM image of Ag@Cl-CQDs (Fig. 1c). The grown Ag nanoparticles have substantially improved the visible light absorption capabilities of Ag@Cl-CQDs hybrids, as shown in Fig. 2a [50]. Further, the growth of Ag nanoparticles has also been reflected in XRD measurements of Cl-CQDs and Ag@Cl-CQDs. The Cl-CQDs exhibited a single broad diffraction peak at 17.3° corresponded to the (002) plane of amorphous carbon-based materials, while diffraction peaks observed for Ag@Cl-CQDs hybrid at 27.9, 32.3, 38.1, 44.3, and 46.3° were, respectively, attributed to (210), (122), (111), (200), and (231) planes of face centered cubic structure of Ag nanoparticles (JCPDS card. No.04-0783) (Figs. 2c and S4). The absence of Cl-CQDs related diffraction peak in the XRD spectrum of Ag@Cl-CQDs was possibly due to the relatively poor crystallinity of Cl-CQDs as compared to Ag. However, Raman spectroscopy measurements of Ag@Cl-CQDs hybrid under the 633 nm excitation clearly revealed the presence of Cl-CQDs (Fig. 2d). The Raman spectrum of Ag@Cl-CQDs exhibited three peaks. Two peaks at 1340 and 1590 cm−1 were corresponded to D and G bands of Cl-CQDs, respectively, while the other peak at 237 cm−1 was due to Ag–O and/or Ag–Cl interactions between Ag nanoparticles and (carboxylic and/or chlorine functional groups of) Cl-CQDs (Fig. 2d) [51,52].

XPS was used to further verify the formation of Ag nanoparticles and to examine the oxidation state of elements and the surface composition of Ag@Cl-CQDs hybrids (Fig. 2e–i). As shown in Fig. 2e, the full-scan XPS spectrum acquired in the binding energy range of 160–600 eV was mainly comprised signals corresponded to Ag (∼2 at. %), C (∼65 at. %), O (∼28 at. %), and Cl (∼5 at. %) elements. The high-resolution XPS spectra in the Ag 3d region exhibited two peaks at binding energies of 368.34 and 374.34 eV corresponded to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 peaks of metallic Ag, respectively (Fig. 2f) [50,53]. The sharpness of Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 peaks ruled out the possibility of Ag+ state co-existence. This concluded that the photoreduction of AgNO3 to Ag nanoparticles was not accompanied by the formation of other Ag-related products or in-situ surface oxidation of Ag nanoparticles to Ag2O [53,54]. Therefore, the observed O, C, and Cl peaks were exclusively related to Cl-CQDs. The presence of Cl was evidenced in the form of two peaks such as Cl 2s peak at 269.92 eV and Cl 2p peak at 199.07 eV (Fig. 2e). The deconvolution of Cl 2p peak revealed the presence of C–Cl and Ag–Cl bonds in Ag@Cl-CQDs hybrid; peaks at 200.18 eV (Cl 2p3/2) and 201.68 eV (Cl 2p1/2) were assigned to C–Cl bonds, while peaks at 198.17 eV (Cl 2p3/2) and 199.67 (Cl 2p1/2) were attributed to Ag–Cl interactions (Fig. 2g) [55–57]. This is possible because two sets of Cl were expected in the developed Ag@Cl-CQDs hybrids, i.e., one set facing the Ag nanoparticle growing in-situ on Cl-CQD surface reasoned to strong Ag–Cl interactions, while the other set from the portion of Cl-CQD (or from the core of the CQD) that was not in interaction with the growing Ag nanoparticle gave rise to C–Cl bonds. On the other hand, the deconvolution of O 1s peak revealed the presence of C–O (533.29 eV) and C=O (532.19 eV) bonds, while peaks observed in the C 1s region specified the existence of C–OH (288.42 eV), C–C (286.54 eV), and C=C (284.81 eV) bonds (Fig. 2h and i) [37,58]. This further confirmed the existence of oxygenic surface functional groups of Cl-CQDs.

To understand the growth mechanism of Ag@Cl-CQDs, the color of aliquots taken from AgNO3/Cl-CQDs solution mixture at different time intervals during the course of 25 min visible light irradiation was examined (Fig. S5). The initial pale yellow color was changed to brown and dark brown color with increasing the visible light irradiation time. This changed color was due to the increased concentration of Ag nanoparticles with increasing the time of light irradiation [59]. Therefore, it was concluded that Cl-CQDs act as template during the growth of Ag nanoparticle and a prolonged reaction could increase the formation of Ag nanoparticles from Ag+ ions (Fig. 1d). During the reaction, electrons were transferred from Cl-CQDs to Ag+ ions (decorated on Cl-CQDs surface) and reduce them to Ag0; reduced Ag0 initiates nucleation and further fused together with other Ag0 to form Ag nanoparticles (Fig. 1d).

Because of the Cl-CQDs or Ag@Cl-CQDs hybrid excellent dispersibility in ethanol or water, a facile solution processing technique, such as drop-casting, was utilized to fabricate the Cl-CQDs or Ag@Cl-CQDs thin film on Pt electrodes coated SiO2/Si substrate for measuring the gas sensor properties at room temperature (Fig. 3 a). The AFM investigations on the surface morphologies of Cl-CQD and Ag@Cl-CQD films revealed that both Cl-CQD and Ag@Cl-CQD structures were packed densely to form continuous films upon drying the drop casted solutions (Fig. S6). However, the Cl-CQD film surface was quite uniform (mean roughness ∼1 nm) as compared to the Ag@Cl-CQD film (mean roughness ∼130 nm) (Fig. S6). Further, the current-voltage characteristics measured under dark condition revealed a ohmic behavior for both Cl-CQD and Ag@Cl-CQD films (Fig. S7). However, the Ag@Cl-CQD film showed a higher current as compared to the Cl-CQD film owing to excellent conductivity of Ag nanoparticles (Fig. S7). The resistance signal measured for the Cl-CQD or Ag@Cl-CQD sensor records a change when the exposed gas changed from H2S to dry air. The resistance of the device decreases in the H2S gas atmosphere and restored back to its original value when the test chamber is flushed with dry air. The response of the sensor device can be estimated by measuring the resistance change, i.e.,

denotes the resistance of the device in dry air and

represents the resistance of the device when the dry air replaced with H2S gas. The response of the fabricated Cl-CQDs and Ag@Cl-CQDs hybrid sensors at various H2S gas concentrations and the influence of UV (254 nm) and visible (457 nm) light irradiation on the gas sensing properties of Ag@Cl-CQDs sensor were shown in Fig. 3b–e. The response of the Cl-CQDs or Ag@Cl-CQDs sensor to H2S gas increased with increasing the gas concentration from 10 ppm to 100 ppm and the Ag@Cl-CQDs hybrid sensor exhibited a better response at all gas concentrations (Fig. 3b and c). The difference in gas response of Cl-CQDs and Ag@Cl-CQDs sensors was more predominant at higher gas concentrations. At 100 ppm of H2S gas concentration, the Ag@Cl-CQDs hybrid exhibited a response of 27.2%, which was 1.62 times higher compared to the response of Cl-CQDs (16.7%) (Fig. 4 a). This suggested that the incorporation of Ag nanoparticles on Cl-CQDs promotes the superior H2S gas sensitivity. Further, as the response and recovery time tends to increase with the raise in difference between Ra and
R
H 2
S
of the device, an increased response and recovery time would be expected for Ag@Cl-CQDs sensor. However, the Ag@Cl-CQDs sensor exhibited faster recovery compared to the Cl-CQDs sensor due to the chemical catalytic effect of the Ag nanoparticles (Fig. 4c and d) [60]. The Ag nanoparticles present within the Ag@Cl-CQDs hybrid can accelerate the adsorption and dissociation of oxygen molecules, and the charged oxygen species (resulted from the dissociation of oxygen molecules) migrated onto Cl-CQDs surface when sensor was exposed to air after its reaction with the target H2S gas molecules. This spill-over effect induced by Ag nanoparticles resulted in fast recovery and increased amount of adsorbed oxygen species on the Ag@Cl-CQDs surface [60].

The UV (254 nm) or visible (457 nm) light irradiation has been displayed a significant influence on the gas response characteristics and response/recovery time of Ag@Cl-CQDs sensor (Fig. 3d and e, and Fig. 4e and f). Notably, UV light wavelength overlapped with the absorption region of Cl-CQDs while the wavelength of visible light matched the Ag nanoparticle absorption region (Fig. 2a). At 100 ppm of H2S gas concentration, the UV and visible light irradiation improved the gas response of Ag@Cl-CQDs sensor to 72.7% and 55.9% (2.67 and 2.05 times to the response observed in the dark condition), respectively (Fig. 4a and b). Clearly, the UV light irradiation yielded superior gas response characteristics from Ag@Cl-CQDs sensor. The interfacial connection between Ag and Cl-CQDs facilitated the efficient migration of UV light induced charge carriers from Cl-CQDs to Ag nanoparticles. These additional charge carriers were known to accelerate the formation of charged oxygen species and thereby the gas response characteristics. Further, the UV light would supply the activation energy for combustion reaction of H2S gas on the surface of sensor [61]. Hence, the quick response and recovery time values were observed for the sensor under UV light irradiation (Fig. 4e and f). Further, the sensor under UV light irradiation continued to exhibit excellent H2S detection ability even under low H2S gas concentrations (2 ppm–200 ppb). Promisingly, the sensor displayed a response of 2.5 and 1% for 500 and 200 ppb of H2S gas, respectively (Fig. 5 a and b). The performance of UV or visible light-activated Ag@Cl-CQDs sensor was comparable with other thermal- or light-activated H2S gas sensors reported in the literature (Table S1). In addition, the sensor displayed an excellent selectivity towards H2S gas in comparison with other gases including NH3, NO2, H2, and CO (Fig. 5c). The response to 100 ppm of H2S gas was 2.9, 3.9, 13.4, and 22.7 times higher as compared to the response observed for 100 ppm of NH3, NO2, H2, and CO gases, respectively (Fig. 5c). The excellent selectivity of Ag@Cl-CQDs sensor towards H2S gas was possibly originated from the Ag nanoparticles affinity to H2S gas molecules.

To demonstrate the reliability of the sensor in real-time application, the response of Ag@Cl-CQDs sensor to 100 ppm H2S gas under humid environment (20%, 40%, and 60%) at room temperature was recorded (Fig. 5d). The increased relative humidity resulted in a reasonable drop in sensor response from a maximum response of 27.2% (at dry atmosphere) to 24.1% (at 20% humidity), 14.8% (at 40% humidity), and 10.1% (at 60% humidity). These results suggested that the moisture interact with the sensor material and further interfere the gas molecules interaction with the sensor material. However, a noteworthy response of 10.1% at higher humidity levels of 60% signified the sensor's reliability and applicability in highly humid environments (Fig. 5d).

The gas sensing mechanism of a material has been predominantly linked to its change in electrical resistance upon surface adsorption and desorption of target gas/oxygen molecules. In the present study, the electrical resistance of the Cl-CQDs sensor device was increased upon its exposure to air–an indication for the n-type conductivity of the Cl-CQDs–due to the trapping of electrons from the conduction band of Cl-CQDs by the surface adsorbed oxygen molecules during the formation of negatively charged oxygen species (
O 2 −
) (eq. (1) and (2)) [62]. Hence, thick charge depletion region developed in the n-type Cl-CQDs. However, when Cl-CQDs sensor was exposed to H2S gas, the H2S molecules interact with the surface adsorbed
O 2 −
species and release a large number of electrons into Cl-CQDs while forming H2O and SO2 gases (eq. (3) and (4)) [62]. Therefore, the depletion region become thin and the electrical resistance of the Cl-CQDs sensor was decreased or restored back to its initial value.

The presence of Ag nanoparticle on the surface of Cl-CQDs played an important role in enhancing the surface reactions and, as a result, promoted the Ag@Cl-CQDs sensor to show better H2S gas response characteristics as compared to the Cl-CQDs sensor. As discussed above and shown in eqs. (1)–(4), the formed
O 2 −
quantities and their subsequent efficient interaction with H2S molecules were significantly important for the higher gas sensing response. Here, the presence of Ag nanoparticles in Ag@Cl-CQDs hybrid was beneficial for the H2S gas sensing in two ways: 1) the high electrical conductivity and catalytic properties of Ag nanoparticle facilitate the enhanced dissociation of oxygen molecules and also result in spilling of the created activated oxygen species over the Ag@Cl-CQDs sensor surface [63] and 2) the strong affinity of Ag nanoparticle to sulfur compounds facilitate them to offer highly specific binding sites for the adsorption of H2S gas molecules [64]. Therefore, Ag@Cl-CQDs sensor exhibited an obvious enhancement in the gas sensing performance (Fig. 6 a).

Under UV (254 nm) or visible (457 nm) light irradiation conditions, the photogenerated electrons and holes in Ag@Cl-CQDs result in a completely different oxygen adsorption kinetics as compared to the conventional oxygen adsorption observed under dark conditions. The surface adsorbed
O 2 −
react with photogenerated holes (
h
p h o t o
+
) and desorb from the sensor surface as O2 (eq. (5)), which facilitates the increased number of reactive sites on the sensor surface (Fig. 6b). The desorbed O2 and pre-existed O2 again capture photogenerated electrons (
e

−
) from the sensor and adsorb as negatively charged molecular oxygen on the surface of sensor (
O
2

) (eq. (6)) (Fig. 6b). These photo-adsorbed
O
2
( p h o t o )
−
species were predicated to be weakly bound on the sensor surface and highly reactive than the conventionally adsorbed
O 2 −
species;
O 2 −
were thermodynamically stable and less reactive at room temperature. Hence, the overall H2S gas response characteristics of Ag@Cl-CQDs sensor accelerated under the light irradiation (eq. (7)). Especially, under the irradiation of UV light, the Cl-CQDs were activated and their excellent charge separation efficiency allowed the transfer of electrons to Ag nanoparticles and holes to react with adsorbed
O 2 −
species (and desorb them as O2) (Fig. 6b). The transferred excited electrons of Cl-CQDs to Ag nanoparticle enabled the formation of more quantities of photo-adsorbed
O
2
( p h o t o )
−
species and thereby enhanced the gas sensing properties. The room temperature PL characteristics measured under 360 nm excitation for Ag@Cl-CQD hybrids at various growth stages ascertained the efficient transfer of electrons from Cl-CQDs to Ag nanoparticles. The initial AgNO3/Cl-CQDs solution mixture exhibited a broad PL emission peak (centered at ∼455 nm) corresponded to Cl-CQDs (Fig. 7 ). However, the PL emission intensity was decreased upon increasing the time of visible light irradiation on solution mixture or the growth time of Ag@Cl-CQDs hybrids. This concluded that the radiative recombination occur in Cl-CQDs under the 360 nm excitation was hindered by the transfer of photoexcited electrons from Cl-CQDs to the grown Ag nanoparticles. Further, the PL emission peak was completely disappeared for the optimum reaction time (25 min) maintained in this work. This indicated that the 25 min of growth time generated the sufficient amount of Ag nanoparticles to capture all photogenerated electrons from Cl-CQDs and thereby enabled the excellent charge separation required for the enhanced gas sensing performance. The excellent charge migration can be attributed to 1) the in-situ growth facilitated strong interfacial connection between Ag and Cl-CQDs and 2) the excellent photogenerated charge carrier separation offered by Cl-CQDs. On the other hand, a completely opposite interfacial charge transfer was estimated to occur under visible light (457 nm) irradiation. Ag nanoparticles, owing to their optical absorption overlapped with the incident visible photon energy, were predominantly activated as compared to Cl-CQDs. Hence, the photogenerated electrons formed by the surface plasmonic effect of Ag nanoparticles were transferred from Ag to Cl-CQDs and, as a result, a comparatively lower gas sensing performance was observed owing to poor oxygen molecules dissociation ability of Cl-CQDs as compared to Ag nanoparticles (Fig. 6b).

In summary, a simple photoelectron transfer assisted reduction of Ag+ ions on the surface of Cl-CQDs was demonstrated to synthesize the Ag@Cl-CQDs hybrid material. The in-situ growth facilitated intimate interfacial contact between Ag and Cl-CQDs allowed the efficient migration of photogenerated electrons through the heterojunction formed between Cl-CQDs and Ag. The excellent oxygen dissociation ability of Ag and superior photocharge separation ability of Cl-CQDs collectively contributed to the enhanced sensing performance of Ag@Cl-CQDs hybrid under the light irradiation. But, the worthy charge separation resulted under UV light irradiation, as compared to under visible light irradiation, yielded a superior response from the Ag@Cl-CQDs sensor towards the H2S gas. The irradiation of UV light produced the photogenerated electrons and holes in Cl-CQDs, where the photoelectrons swiftly migrated to Ag nanoparticles for dissociating more quantities of oxygen molecules while the photoholes combine with the adsorbed oxygen species to desorb them for creating more active sites. Hence, UV light irradiation escalated the gas sensing performance of Ag@Cl-CQDs hybrid material significantly. Further, the detection limit of Ag@Cl-CQDs under the irradiation of UV light extended from ppm to ppb levels of H2S gas. These newly uncovered results by utilizing the combination of CQDs and plasmonic metal nanoparticles for H2S gas sensing provide insights to progressive light-activated gas sensors research.
